speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by checking your vitals. Your diastolic blood pressure today is 95 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: Good morning, Doctor. Thanks for seeing me. I’ve been feeling a bit lightheaded when standing up lately.

speaker: doctor
text: I see — your heart rate is 64 bpm, which is within normal range. That’s reassuring, though we’ll keep an eye on orthostatic symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, it happens mostly in the mornings. I also noticed my feet feel a little tingly sometimes.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — we’ll assess for possible peripheral neuropathy. How has your blood sugar been trending?

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Insulin glargine 10 units nightly — let’s review how that’s working and whether adjustments are needed.

speaker: patient
text: My fasting sugars have been around 130–150 mg/dL, but I had one reading of 182 last Thursday before breakfast.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure today is 103 mmHg — slightly lower than usual, but still in acceptable range given your age and treatment goals.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin level is 169 g/L — well within normal limits, so no anemia concerns at this time.

speaker: doctor
text: As we discussed previously, your primary diagnosis remains Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, with ongoing focus on glycemic and cardiovascular risk management.

speaker: patient
text: Right — I’ve been trying to walk more and cut back on carbs. But I’m worried about the dizziness getting worse.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s consider whether your insulin dose or timing might be contributing. Also, do you check your BP at home?

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I use my wrist monitor every morning before meds. It usually reads between 100 and 110 systolic.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful. We’ll also screen for autonomic involvement — orthostatic hypotension could explain the lightheadedness.

speaker: patient
text: I did notice my BP dropped a bit more yesterday — I think it was around 90 systolic when I stood up quickly.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s notable. Let’s recheck your seated and standing pressures now to assess for orthostasis.

speaker: patient
text: Sure — I’ll sit here while you get the cuff ready.

speaker: doctor
text: Interesting — your seated systolic is now 90 mmHg. That’s lower than the 103 we recorded earlier this visit. We’ll need to investigate possible over-treatment or volume status.

speaker: patient
text: Hmm — maybe it’s the new water pill my cardiologist added two weeks ago? I forgot to mention it.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked again just now, it’s back to 103 — that number matches what you got earlier.
